title: On Adjoints and Dual Matroids
overall_summary: The paper studies lattice-theoretic adjoints of geometric lattices associated to matroids and compares this notion with ordinary set-theoretic matroid duality. Its main result is a counterexample to a question of Mason: the rank-3 non-Desargues matroid has an adjoint, but its ordinary dual matroid does not. The core obstruction is Cheung's embedding theorem, which says that the existence of an adjoint forces an order-preserving embedding of the adjoint lattice into the extension lattice of modular filters; this implies a Levi-type intersection property for copoints. The counterexample proves failure of this Levi property by exhibiting six hyperplanes in the dual non-Desargues matroid whose generated modular filter is already the top filter, i.e. any point extension constrained to lie on all six hyperplanes makes the new point a loop. For the original problem, the most useful import is the strategy of deriving a necessary intersection/generation property from an order-reversing lattice duality and then disproving extension by a finite incidence obstruction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition: adjoint of a geometric lattice
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a geometric lattice. A geometric lattice L^A is called an adjoint of L if rank(L^A) = rank(L) and there exists an order-reversing injective map φ: L -> L^A such that points, i.e. rank-one elements, of L are mapped into copoints, i.e. corank-one elements, of L^A, and copoints of L are mapped onto the points of L^A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition introduced in the paper following Cheung's terminology. The paper emphasizes that this is a lattice-theoretic notion of duality and is distinct from ordinary matroid duality, where bases are sent to set-theoretic complements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1 observations: modular, linear, and rank-three geometric lattices have adjoints
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Every modular geometric lattice L has an adjoint, namely the order-dual lattice obtained by reversing the order relation. More generally, if L is a linear geometric lattice, meaning that its elements are the linear subspaces spanned by a finite set of points in a projective space P, then L has an adjoint obtained by embedding L into the modular lattice P, applying the anti-isomorphism P -> P^op, and then applying an appropriate retraction. Also, every rank-3 geometric lattice has an adjoint; the paper notes that one may first construct a possibly infinite modular extension by successively intersecting lines, and then use the same embedding/anti-isomorphism/retraction construction.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a modular geometric lattice, reversal of the order relation already gives a geometric lattice of the same rank with the required order-reversing correspondence. For a linear lattice L sitting inside a projective-space lattice P, the projective-space lattice is modular and has the usual anti-isomorphism to its order dual; composing the inclusion L -> P, this anti-isomorphism, and a retraction back to a suitable lattice gives the desired order-reversing injective map sending points to copoints and copoints to points. For rank 3, the paper asserts that one can build a modular extension by repeatedly adding intersections of lines; after embedding the original rank-3 lattice into this modular extension, the same construction yields an adjoint.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Point-extension/modular-filter correspondence and the extension lattice E(L)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a geometric lattice with top element 1_L. A modular filter in L is a subset F of L satisfying: if x is in F and y >= x, then y is in F; and if x,y are in F and x,y form a modular pair, then x ∧ y is in F. The paper recalls the standard one-to-one correspondence between single-element point extensions L ∪ p of L and modular filters F of L: the filter F consists of those elements of L whose image in the extension contains the new point p. The set E(L) of all modular filters of L, ordered by inclusion, is a lattice called the extension lattice. It has unique minimum element {1_L}, corresponding to adding a point in general position, and unique maximum element L, corresponding to a loop extension. The atoms, or minimal nonzero elements, of E(L) are exactly the filters {H, 1_L} where H is a copoint of L; these atoms are called the points of E(L), so points of E(L) are in bijection with copoints of L.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this as the standard correspondence between point extensions and modular filters, referring to earlier sources. It does not prove the correspondence in detail, but it explains the interpretation: specifying which original flats contain the new point p gives an upward-closed set, and the modular-pair closure condition is precisely what makes this specification compatible with the lattice axioms for a single-element extension. Inclusion of filters orders extensions by the containment constraints imposed on p. The minimum filter {1_L} imposes only that p lies in the top flat, while the maximum filter L forces p to lie in every flat and hence makes p a loop.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1: Cheung's embedding theorem
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L and L^A be geometric lattices of the same rank. If L^A is an adjoint of L, then there exists an injective order-preserving map ε: L^A -> E(L) into the extension lattice of L such that ε maps the points of L^A onto the points of E(L).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper uses this result as its main tool and cites it to Cheung. No proof is given in the paper. The key content for later use is that an order-reversing adjoint map L -> L^A can be converted into an order-preserving embedding of L^A into the modular-filter lattice E(L), and the copoint-to-point part of the adjoint definition becomes the assertion that the points of L^A exactly cover the point filters {H,1_L} of E(L).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.2: existence of an adjoint implies Levi's property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let L be a geometric lattice of rank r. The paper says that L has Levi's property if the maximum element 1_{E(L)} of the extension lattice E(L), equivalently the modular filter L, is not the join of fewer than r points of E(L). Equivalently, the filter L is not generated by fewer than r atom filters {H,1_L}; geometrically, for every set of r-1 copoints H_1,...,H_{r-1} of L, one can add a new point p in a point extension L ∪ p such that p lies on all H_i without being forced to be a loop. Corollary 2.2 states: if L has an adjoint, then L has Levi's property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let L^A be an adjoint of L and let ε: L^A -> E(L) be Cheung's order-preserving injective map from Proposition 2.1, sending points of L^A onto points of E(L). Take any k < r points p_1,...,p_k of E(L). By surjectivity on points, choose points q_1,...,q_k of L^A with ε(q_i)=p_i. Since L^A has rank r, the join x = q_1 ∨ ... ∨ q_k is strictly below the top element 1_{L^A}. Because ε is order-preserving, each p_i = ε(q_i) is at most ε(x), hence p_1 ∨ ... ∨ p_k <= ε(x) in E(L). Since x < 1_{L^A} and ε is injective and order-preserving, ε(x) is a proper element of E(L), so the join of p_1,...,p_k is not the top element of E(L). Thus no fewer than r point filters generate the maximum modular filter L, which is exactly Levi's property.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1: six hyperplanes in the dual non-Desargues matroid force a loop extension
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the non-Desargues rank-3 matroid on the 10-element ground set S labelled as in the paper's Figure 1, with p the center of perspective and {x,y,z} the broken 3-point line. Let H_1,H_2,H_3 be the three 3-point lines of the triangle abc, and let H_4,H_5,H_6 be the three 3-point lines paa', pbb', pcc'. For each i, let H_i^* = S \ H_i, which is a hyperplane of the ordinary dual matroid M^*. Let F^* be the modular filter of the flat lattice of M^* generated by H_1^*,...,H_6^*. Let N^* := M^* ∪ q be the point extension of M^* corresponding to F^*. Then q is a loop in N^*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let N be the ordinary dual of N^*. Since N^* is a point extension of M^*, deleting q from N^* gives M^*, and therefore contracting q in N gives M, i.e. N/q = M. The proof uses the standard dual rank identity: for a dual pair on ground set T, r_N(T \ A) = |T \ A| - r_{N^*}(T) + r_{N^*}(A). In N^*, the new point q lies on each hyperplane H_i^*, so r_{N^*}(H_i^* ∪ q)=6 for i=1,...,6, while r_{N^*}(S ∪ q)=7. Applying the rank identity with T=S∪q and A=H_i^*∪q gives r_N(H_i)=|H_i|-7+6=3-7+6=2. Hence all six chosen H_i remain 3-point lines in N. From the lines paa' and pbb', together with the side line of triangle abc containing a,b,x, it follows that {p,a,a',b,b',x} lies in a plane E of N. Since N/q=M and {x,a',b'} is a line of M, the set {x,a',b',q} spans a plane E' of N. The intersection E ∩ E' contains {x,a',b'}, and the paper concludes that {x,a',b'} is a line in N. The same argument gives that {y,b',c'} and {z,a',c'} are lines in N. Thus all 3-point lines of M remain 3-point lines in N. Considering the three side lines of triangle abc shows that {a,b,c,x,y,z} is coplanar in N; similarly, the corresponding primed-side lines show that {a',b',c',x,y,z} is coplanar. Since {x,y,z} spans a plane in M, and hence in N, these two coplanar sets lie in the same plane, so S \ {p} is coplanar. Finally, because paa' is a line and a,a' are already in this plane, p also lies in it, so all of S is a plane in N. Since N has rank 4 and S has rank 3, this means q is a coloop in N, equivalently q is a loop in N^*.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main counterexample: ordinary duality need not preserve existence of adjoints
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be the rank-3 non-Desargues matroid from Figure 1 of the paper. Then M has an adjoint, but its ordinary dual matroid M^* does not have an adjoint. Consequently, the answer to Mason's question, whether M^* must have an adjoint whenever M has one, is negative.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The matroid M has rank 3, and Section 1 explains that every rank-3 geometric lattice has an adjoint. The dual M^* has rank 7 because M has rank 3 on a 10-element ground set. By Proposition 3.1, the modular filter generated by the six hyperplanes H_1^*,...,H_6^* of M^* is the whole flat lattice of M^*: the corresponding point extension forces the new point to be a loop. In the extension lattice E(M^*), this says that the top element is the join of the six point filters associated with these six hyperplanes. Since six is fewer than rank(M^*)=7, M^* fails Levi's property. By Corollary 2.2, any geometric lattice with an adjoint must have Levi's property, so M^* cannot have an adjoint.

proof_techniques.name: Encode point extensions by modular filters
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces single-element point extensions by modular filters in the flat lattice. In this encoding, requiring a new point p to lie on a copoint H corresponds to including the atom filter {H,1_L} in the generated modular filter. The join of such atoms in the extension lattice E(L) represents the strongest constraints forced by requiring p to lie on the chosen copoints; if that join is the top filter L, the only possible extension makes p a loop.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a poset such as the relative Dressian, this suggests looking for an analogous lattice/poset of extensions or subobjects where flat-induced elements generate a maximal or minimal object. A hypothetical order-reversing involution extending a flat polarity may force certain generated joins or meets not to collapse; finding a collapse would obstruct the extension.

proof_techniques.name: Use an embedding theorem to convert anti-duality into a positive-order obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Cheung's theorem turns the existence of an order-reversing adjoint L -> L^A into an order-preserving injection L^A -> E(L) that is surjective on points. This is powerful because rank constraints in L^A then impose join constraints in E(L): fewer than r points in a rank-r lattice cannot join to the top, and their images therefore cannot generate the top modular filter.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A similar strategy for the original question would be to show that an order-reversing involution of Dr(M) extending the flat-lattice involution induces an order-preserving embedding of some known rank-controlled subposet into an extension or interval poset. Rank or atom-count constraints in that subposet could then become testable join/meet constraints in Dr(M).

proof_techniques.name: Levi-property contrapositive as a nonexistence certificate
proof_techniques.description: Rather than trying to classify all possible adjoints, the paper proves nonexistence by violating a necessary property. Corollary 2.2 says that an adjoint forces Levi's property: any r-1 copoints must be simultaneously intersectable by a nonloop point extension. Therefore, to disprove existence of an adjoint for a rank-r lattice, it suffices to exhibit r-1 copoints whose associated atom filters generate the top of E(L).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Dr(M), one could try to formulate a necessary intersection property forced by an order-reversing involution and then search for a small family of flat-induced tropical linear spaces whose meet/join behavior violates it. This is especially plausible because the original problem already contains a natural flat subposet L(M)^op inside Dr(M).

proof_techniques.name: Dualize a point-extension constraint into a primal coextension rank chase
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 3.1, the paper studies a point extension N^* of M^* by passing to the ordinary dual N. The condition that q lies on specified hyperplanes H_i^* of M^* becomes, via the dual rank formula, the condition that the complementary 3-point sets H_i remain rank-2 lines in N. This converts an abstract modular-filter generation problem into an incidence-geometric problem inside a rank-4 coextension N with N/q=M.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem is attacked through valuated matroids or tropical linear spaces associated with minors, this technique suggests translating inclusion constraints under duality/contraction into concrete rank or incidence constraints on complementary flats. It may help turn a hypothetical involution into verifiable combinatorial conditions on M.

proof_techniques.name: Incidence propagation from a small enforced configuration
proof_techniques.description: The non-Desargues proof starts from six enforced lines in the coextension N and propagates them through plane intersections to force additional 3-point lines to remain lines. Once enough lines are forced, two large coplanar sets sharing the spanning set {x,y,z} must coincide, so the entire original ground set S becomes a plane. This forces the new element q to be a coloop in the dual coextension N, equivalently a loop in the original point extension N^*.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For candidate matroids with a flat-lattice polarity, a finite incidence subconfiguration may be enough to force collapse of an attempted extension of the polarity. Non-Desargues-type configurations are natural test cases for finding obstructions because a small number of imposed incidences can force global degeneracy.

other_useful_info: The paper stresses that lattice-theoretic adjoints and ordinary matroid duals are distinct notions and that their compatibility is not automatic; the main counterexample shows actual incompatibility. It notes that matroids with adjoints properly generalize linear matroids: every linear geometric lattice has an adjoint, and every rank-3 geometric lattice has an adjoint, but some matroids have no adjoint. It also notes that the converse to Cheung's embedding theorem was proved in Bachem and Kern, Extension equivalence of oriented matroids, European J. Combin. 7 (1986), 193-197. Other cited pointers relevant to adjoints and extensions include Cheung, Adjoints of a geometry, Canadian Math. Bull. 17 (1974), 363-365; Bachem and Wanka, Matroids without adjoints; Crapo and Rota on combinatorial geometries; Las Vergnas on point extensions of oriented geometries; and Cordovil on polarity and point extensions in oriented matroids.
why_relevant: Studies the relationship between ordinary matroid duality and lattice-theoretic adjoints, including negative examples. Useful for anticipating obstructions to extending a flat-lattice polarity to a larger quotient poset.
